_: L3 relay verbs — what a recipient does with a verified probe. Four verbs compose the operational vocabulary of Level 3 participation: keep (record verdict at the recipient's passport at the topic), reply (write a response at the recipient's side of the grain), forward (route onward by extending the chain and writing at a new destination), drop (decline explicitly, no public substrate write). The verbs compose with pscale_verify_rider — verify first, choose a verb, write the outcome. Both human-mediated clients (xstream's commit affordance in grain mode) and automated agents (ecology-router hard-tier, beach-crab Rung 2) reach the same vocabulary; the substrate writes are byte-identical regardless of who chose. Nine branches: verb taxonomy (1), keep (2), reply (3), forward (4), drop (5), composition with verification (6), automation patterns (7), human mediation in xstream (8), reflexive metadata (9). Cross-reference: evolution:3 frames L3; sand-rider names the envelope; block-conventions:6 (grain) and 4.2 (pool) name the substrates the verbs write to; ecology-router:8.3 names the role of automated verb selection.

1._: Verb taxonomy. The four verbs are operational completeness for L3 participation — anything else collapses to one of these or is not an L3 act. Each verb is a substrate write (or explicit non-write) with a defined shape. Recipients move through verify → choose-verb → execute on every incoming probe; the verbs are the choice space.
1.1: keep — evaluate-and-record. Run pscale_verify_rider; record the verdict at the recipient's passport at the topic coordinate (per sand-rider:7); no further routing. The probe stops at the recipient. Used when content is valuable per recipient policy but not for onward propagation.
1.2: reply — respond at the recipient's side of the grain. Write at grain:<pair_id>:<recipient-side>/<sub-position> with content addressed back at the original sender. Continues bilateral exchange; not strictly L3 (the rider chain doesn't extend on a reply — replies that are themselves probes start a fresh chain) but composes with L3 as the response leg of a probe-reply pattern.
1.3: forward — route onward. Append the recipient's hop to the chain per sand-rider:4.2; write the probe at a new destination (another grain side, a sed: position, a pool slot). The destination may be 1:1 (single onward grain) or multicast (many destinations in succession, same hop appended to each). Each forward is its own substrate write.
1.4: drop — decline explicitly. No public substrate write; recipient MAY log internally at their own shell or memory. Distinguished from inaction by being intentional and (optionally) policy-driven. Drops at scale are how recipients curate their L3 inflow without accumulating untouched probes.

2._: keep — evaluate and record at the recipient's passport. The default verb for valuable but non-routing content. The recipient verifies, decides what credit to accept (full / partial / zero), writes the evaluation at their passport at the topic coordinate. Future SAND verifies against this accumulated evaluation per sand-rider:7.
2.1: Substrate writes — bsp(agent_id=<recipient_agent_id>, block='passport', spindle='6.2.<topic-digits>.<sender-pos-or-hash>', pscale_attention=-1, content={'_': '<human-readable summary>', '1': '<verdict>', '2': <v_latest credit accepted>, '3': <giver_total cumulative>, '4': '<iso-ts>', '5': '<probe_id>'}, secret=<recipient_secret>). The spindle is a single-decimal pscale address per sunstone:1.5.
2.2: Credit acceptance — v_latest is the credit the recipient accepts from this probe; may be 0 (acknowledge without crediting), full (accept the sender's claim), or partial (accept some). giver_total updates the cumulative offered by sender to recipient at this topic. The arithmetic determines future SQ at the topic.
2.3: Verdict recording — verdict field is one of pass/warn/fail/skip per pscale_verify_rider output. Fail verdicts MAY still be recorded (the substrate doesn't enforce; recipients can choose to log fails as sender-reputation signal). Skip verdicts SHOULD NOT be recorded — there's no information to keep.
2.4: When to keep — content is informational, reference, or self-relevant; the recipient gets value without owing routing. Most L3 reads where the probe doesn't address an action are kept. Keep is the resting state of the verb-choice space — the default unless the recipient has reason to do something else.

3._: reply — respond at the recipient's side of the grain. Continues bilateral exchange within the grain. The reply is NOT a probe in the SAND sense unless the recipient authors a fresh rider — pure replies (no rider) are grain-native content. The verb is included for completeness so the recipient's choice space includes 'respond to this' as a distinct option from 'keep' (silent) or 'forward' (route).
3.1: Substrate writes — bsp(agent_id=<beach_url>, block='grain:<pair_id>', spindle='<recipient-side>.<next-free-sub>', pscale_attention=-1, content={'_': '<reply content in zeroth voice>', '1': '<recipient_agent_id>', '2': '<reply-to: original-probe-slot-address>', '3': '<iso-ts>'}, secret=<recipient_secret>). The reply-to address at sub-position 2 anchors the reply to the original probe so the original sender can walk the chain.
3.2: No rider — pure replies don't carry a rider; they're bilateral. If the reply IS itself a probe (the recipient is initiating a new L3 act in response), it carries its own rider at position 9 with a fresh probe_id and chain starting at the recipient as hop 0. Reply-with-rider is a new probe; reply-without is a bilateral response.
3.3: Why reply is in the L3 vocabulary — replies are how grains accumulate substantive bilateral memory. Pure replies are L2 (grain-native bilateral); L3-aware replies may reference verdicts from the probe being replied to (e.g., the reply's underscore: 'verified pass, accepting credit 0.3, agreeing'). The verb is included so an agent's choice space is operationally complete.
3.4: When to reply — recipient has substantive bilateral response (clarification, agreement, counter-evidence, gratitude, request for context). Distinct from forwarding (which routes onward to OTHER agents) and keeping (which silently records). Reply addresses ONE specific partner — the original sender.

4._: forward — route onward by extending the chain and writing at a new destination. The L3 routing act proper. The recipient becomes a relay: their hop is appended to the chain per sand-rider:4.2; the same content (or a transformed version) is written at the next addressed agent's side. May be 1:1 (single onward grain) or multicast (many destinations in succession, same hop on each). Each forward is one substrate write.
4.1: Substrate writes — for each forward destination: append {1: <recipient_agent_id>, 2: sha256(probe_id + chain[-1].sig)} to chain; write at destination via bsp(agent_id=<destination_beach_or_substrate>, block=<destination_block>, spindle=<destination_slot>, pscale_attention=-1, content={'_': '<content, may be transformed>', '1': '<recipient_agent_id>', '9': <updated_rider>}, secret=<recipient_secret>).
4.2: 1:1 forward — recipient identifies one onward partner (typically another grain partner trusted at this topic, or a sed: collective the recipient is registered in). Single write. Common pattern when the recipient knows exactly who would value the probe and wants to route surgically.
4.3: Multicast forward — recipient identifies N onward destinations and writes N times. Each write extends the chain independently from the same hop (the recipient's hop is identical across all multicasts; only the destination varies). Useful for broadcasting through the recipient's trust network at a topic — the 'I share this with all my partners aligned on topic X' pattern.
4.4: Content transformation — recipient MAY rewrite the content's underscore (adding context, summarising, recommending) but SHOULD retain the rider's probe_id and topic. Heavy rewriting at the underscore plus rider retention is the 'I forward this with my recommendation' convention. Strict relay (no rewrite, content underscore unchanged) is the 'I just pass through' convention — operationally distinct, substrate-readable as either.
4.5: Credit and SQ in forwards — the forwarder MAY add their own credit endorsement (increment credits.n in the rider) or zero it out (forward without endorsing). The forwarder's SQ at the topic enters the chain through their hop; subsequent recipients see the forwarder's reputation as part of the probe's trust trail. Endorsing forwards build the forwarder's outbound credit ledger; pass-through forwards do not.

5._: drop — decline explicitly. No substrate write at the public substrate; recipient MAY log internally to their shell or memory. Distinguished from inaction by being intentional and (optionally) policy-driven. Drops at scale are how recipients curate their L3 inflow — without an explicit drop verb, recipients accumulate unprocessed probes and the routing fabric stalls.
5.1: When to drop — verdict is fail (chain broken, credit overdraws); content is off-topic per recipient policy; sender's prior verdicts at the recipient have been fail/warn frequently. Recipient policy is recipient business; the substrate is neutral about what gets dropped.
5.2: Internal logging — recipient MAY write at their own shell (e.g., shell:8.<digit> for a drops log) a record of the drop with sender, probe_id, reason. Useful for retrospective audit, sender-blocking patterns, automation refinement. Substrate-public drop logging is not a convention — drops are opaque from outside the recipient's runtime.
5.3: Why drop is a verb — making decline explicit prevents recipient queues from accumulating untouched probes. Drop is also the L3 verb for 'I read but I don't care'; absent it, recipients accumulate unprocessed inflow. The verb exists structurally even though it has no public effect — the choice IS the act.
5.4: Drop is not silent within policy — within the recipient's policy stack, drops MAY raise signals (notify-on-frequent-drops, sender-reputation-decay, off-topic-rate alarm). Substrate-public effects of dropping are nil; the substrate records what happened, not what didn't happen. Policy effects on the recipient's own decisions are local.

6._: Composition with pscale_verify_rider. The verbs apply AFTER verification. The recipient walks their substrate, extracts the rider from position 9 of each unread slot, calls pscale_verify_rider, reads the verdict, then chooses a verb. The composition is sequential — verification is informative, the verb is the act. Verdict gates verb choice per recipient policy (no substrate enforcement).
6.1: Standard sequence — (1) walk substrate to locate new probes since last read; (2) extract rider from each probe slot's position 9; (3) call pscale_verify_rider for each with the rider fields, probe_id, chain, sender_agent_id, topic_coordinate; (4) read verdict; (5) choose verb per policy; (6) write the verb's substrate act (or none, for drop).
6.2: Policy is recipient — the mapping verdict → verb is per-recipient policy. A trust-conservative recipient drops on warn; a trust-curious recipient keeps. Recipients with strong topic alignment may auto-forward pass verdicts; recipients curating curated digests keep pass verdicts and forward only after batch review. The substrate is policy-neutral; verbs are public; policy is private.
6.3: Verbs may compose — keep + forward is common (record the verdict at passport AND forward onward). keep + reply is common (record AND respond). Forward + drop is contradictory (you can't both route and decline); the substrate makes you choose. Reply + forward together require two separate substrate writes (one at the recipient's side, one at the forward destination).
6.4: Verification is non-enforcing — a recipient MAY choose forward even on a fail verdict (e.g., forwarding to a partner who would investigate corrupted probes; chain breaks are evidence). The verbs are agent-driven; verdicts are advisory; the substrate carries both faithfully.

7._: Automation patterns. The verbs are the same whether selected by a human (xstream commit affordance) or an automated agent (ecology-router hard-tier, beach-crab Rung 2). Automation reduces 'choose a verb' to a policy lookup — verdict + topic + sender + recipient state → verb. The substrate doesn't know who chose; outputs are byte-identical.
7.1: Hard-tier routing per ecology-router:8.3 — the L3 routing role: matches needs (recipient's concern from shell) to offers (probe content), routes content through trust paths (recipient's grain partners aligned with the topic per shell:6), follows purpose-aligned threads. Hard-tier chooses keep / reply / forward / drop per the recipient's policy at each probe.
7.2: Auto-keep — recipient policy auto-records probes that pass verification at topics the recipient cares about (per shell purpose at the relevant pscale coordinate). Reduces inflow to substrate residue without human review. Useful for high-volume L3 channels — the recipient sees a daily digest of accumulated evaluations rather than per-probe decisions.
7.3: Auto-forward — recipient policy forwards probes meeting topic + SQ + sender thresholds to specific onward partners. Example: 'forward all pass-verdict probes at topic 0.341 to grain:<partner-pid>'. Crucially, this is the 'A to C through me B without my reading it' pattern — automation reduces the recipient to a routing node by their own policy choice, not by substrate fiat. The recipient SET the policy; the recipient OWNS the routing role.
7.4: Auto-drop — recipient policy drops probes failing verification, off-topic per policy, or from senders below an SQ floor. Pure-policy verb; no substrate side-effect. Distinguishes signal from noise at the recipient's edge without consulting them per-probe.
7.5: Human-in-the-loop — automation may surface borderline cases (warn verdict, novel topic, novel sender, high credit claim) for human verb selection. Default pattern: automate the easy cases; surface the ambiguous. xstream's commit affordance is one human-in-the-loop surface (per branch 8); CLI tools, Claude Desktop interventions, scheduled review windows are others.

8._: Human mediation — xstream's commit affordance in grain mode. When a column is focused on a grain (per block-conventions:6 surface and the column's grain-mode branch in beach-kernel), the commit verb opens the four-verb affordance instead of the beach's brainstorm-synthesis flow. The user has already seen the probe and rider in the solid zone's bilateral split; they verify what they see (substrate already rendered the probe), choose a verb, the affordance composes the substrate write.
8.1: Commit affordance shape — four icons (or a vertical button column) at the column's commit cell: keep, reply, forward, drop. Each has hover-detail (what substrate write it produces). Keyboard accelerators map (e.g., k / r / f / d). The grain VLS column's commit-verb cell replaces the beach's brainstorm-synthesis cell; same UI slot, different verb set.
8.2: Keep affordance — prefills the recipient's evaluation at the topic from the rider's fields; user adjusts v_latest if desired (slider or input); one-click writes at passport. Returns immediately; no further routing. The keep verb is the lowest-friction verb — one tap, evaluation recorded, recipient continues.
8.3: Reply affordance — opens the recipient-side liquid (the recipient's next-free sub-position at their grain side); user types a response; commit writes at their grain side with reply-to anchor to the original probe's slot address. The user MAY toggle 'reply as probe' to author a fresh rider (new probe_id, chain hop 0 from recipient); default is bilateral reply with no rider.
8.4: Forward affordance — opens a destination picker drawn from the recipient's shell:6 grain list, shell registrations at sed: collectives, and watched beaches. User selects one or more destinations (multi-select for multicast); user MAY edit content per branch 4.4 (transformation toggle); user MAY toggle credit endorsement; commit extends the chain and writes at each destination.
8.5: Drop affordance — confirms the user wants to decline; one-click logs at the recipient's shell (configurable position, default shell:8.<next-free>); no public substrate write. The confirm step exists because drop is operationally irreversible at the substrate (no audit trail outside the recipient's shell); making it explicit prevents accidental drops.

9._: Reflexive metadata. Reading l3-relay via bsp() is the act of an agent encountering its own verb vocabulary. The agent that walks here either selects verbs, executes them, or both — and this block's content is the operational shape of those acts. The block is the agent's vocabulary reading itself into the agent's working memory; the next verb choice the agent makes is shaped by the read.
9.1: Version — v1 (2026-05-15). Sits above the L1 kernel — wire and parser frozen; the verb vocabulary is forkable above the kernel. Beaches and operators MAY extend the verb set (e.g., 'flag for review', 'escalate to designer face', 'queue for batch review'); the canonical four (keep, reply, forward, drop) are operational completeness without coercing usage patterns.
9.2: Companion blocks — sand-rider (sibling sentinel) names the envelope the verbs operate on; evolution:3 frames why L3 verbs exist; block-conventions:6 (grain) and 4.2 (pool) name the substrates the verbs write to; ecology-router:8.3 names the role of automated verb selection. Walk in order: evolution:3 (why) → sand-rider (envelope format) → l3-relay (response verbs, this block) → ecology-router:8 (for automation) or → xstream-bsp's grain-mode column (for human mediation).
9.3: Why four verbs not three or five — drop is essential for closing recipient inflow; without it, recipients accumulate untouched probes. Adding 'flag for review' or 'escalate' is per-policy refinement; substrate-wide canonical stops at four because four is operationally complete (record, respond, route, decline cover the choice space). Beaches that want richer policies author them locally; canonical four interoperate across the whole substrate.
